title: Yangnyeom Korean Fried Chicken
description: Yangnyeom Korean Fried Chicken is a symphony of textures and flavors, featuring impossibly crispy chicken pieces coated in a sticky, sweet, spicy, and savory glaze. This beloved Korean dish offers a perfect balance of heat from gochujang, tang from vinegar, and richness from garlic and soy, making every bite an addictive experience.

ingredients.notes: adjust to desired spice level, for sauce

instructions.title: Marinate the Chicken
instructions.text: Pat the chicken pieces thoroughly dry with paper towels. In a large bowl, combine the chicken with 1 tbsp soy sauce, 1 tbsp mirin, 1 tsp grated ginger, and 1/2 tsp black pepper. Mix well to ensure each piece is coated. Cover and refrigerate for at least 20 minutes, or up to an hour, to allow flavors to meld.

instructions.title: Prepare the Dredging Batter
instructions.text: In a separate large bowl, whisk together the potato starch, all-purpose flour, baking powder, and 1/2 tsp salt. Gradually add the cold water, starting with 1/2 cup, and mix until a thin, smooth batter forms. The consistency should be like a thin pancake batter, just enough to lightly coat the chicken without being too thick. Add more water if needed.

instructions.title: Cook the Yangnyeom Sauce
instructions.text: In a medium saucepan, combine gochujang, gochugaru, 1/4 cup soy sauce, rice syrup, sugar, 2 tbsp mirin, minced garlic, grated ginger, sesame oil, rice vinegar, and 1/4 cup water. Whisk everything together until smooth. Bring the mixture to a gentle simmer over medium-low heat, stirring occasionally, until it thickens slightly, about 5-7 minutes. Remove from heat and set aside.

instructions.title: First Fry the Chicken
instructions.text: Pour neutral frying oil into a deep pot or Dutch oven, ensuring it's at least 3 inches deep. Heat the oil to 325°F (160°C). Dip each marinated chicken piece into the prepared batter, letting any excess drip off. Carefully lower the chicken into the hot oil in batches, ensuring not to overcrowd the pot. Fry for 6-7 minutes until the chicken is cooked through and lightly golden. Remove with a slotted spoon and place on a wire rack set over a baking sheet to drain. Repeat with remaining chicken.

instructions.title: Second Fry for Ultimate Crispiness
instructions.text: Increase the oil temperature to 350°F (175°C). Once the oil is at temperature, return the chicken pieces to the hot oil for a second fry, again in batches. Fry for another 3-4 minutes, or until the chicken is deeply golden brown and incredibly crispy. This double-frying technique is crucial for achieving the signature Korean fried chicken crunch. Remove the chicken and place it back on the wire rack to drain any excess oil.

instructions.title: Coat and Serve
instructions.text: In a large mixing bowl, add the freshly fried, crispy chicken. Pour the warm yangnyeom sauce over the chicken. Using tongs or a spatula, gently toss the chicken until each piece is evenly coated in the sticky, flavorful sauce. Garnish generously with toasted sesame seeds and chopped scallions. Serve immediately and enjoy the perfect balance of sweet, spicy, and savory flavors.

tips: For the crispiest chicken, ensure your oil temperatures are accurate. A kitchen thermometer is highly recommended for both frying stages.
Do not overcrowd the frying pot. Frying in smaller batches helps maintain oil temperature and ensures even cooking and crispiness.
The secret to a light, crispy Korean fried chicken coating is often potato starch. While cornstarch works, potato starch tends to yield a slightly lighter and crispier result.
Coat the chicken in the sauce just before serving to maintain maximum crispiness. If coated too early, the chicken may become soggy.
article:
## History of Yangnyeom Korean Fried Chicken

Korean fried chicken, affectionately known as 'chikin' (치킨), has a relatively modern but vibrant history. Its origins can be traced back to the post-Korean War era when American soldiers introduced deep-fried chicken to the peninsula. However, it was in the 1970s and 80s that Korean chefs began to innovate, moving beyond simple salt-and-pepper coatings. The true game-changer was the invention of 'yangnyeom' (양념) sauce, a sweet, spicy, and savory glaze that perfectly complemented the crispy fried chicken. This innovation sparked a culinary revolution, leading to the proliferation of specialized fried chicken restaurants across South Korea and cementing its status as a national obsession, often enjoyed with beer (chimaek culture).

## Why This Recipe Works

This recipe achieves authentic Yangnyeom Korean Fried Chicken through a combination of traditional techniques and carefully balanced flavors. The key is the double-frying method: a lower temperature first fry cooks the chicken thoroughly, while a second, higher temperature fry renders the fat from the skin and creates an incredibly light, airy, and shatteringly crisp crust. The use of potato starch in the batter further enhances this unique texture. The yangnyeom sauce, with its precise blend of gochujang, rice syrup, garlic, and ginger, strikes a perfect harmony between sweetness, heat, umami, and a touch of tang, ensuring every bite is intensely flavorful and addictive without overwhelming the delicate crunch of the chicken.

## Chef Tips for Perfection

To elevate your Yangnyeom Korean Fried Chicken, always start with well-dried chicken; excess moisture leads to less crispy results. When creating the batter, aim for a thin consistency – a light coating is what makes the crust so unique. Monitor your oil temperatures diligently; too cold, and the chicken will be greasy; too hot, and it will burn before cooking through. For the sauce, don't over-thicken it; it should be glossy and coat the back of a spoon, not be a heavy paste. Lastly, ensure you toss the chicken in the sauce only just before serving. This preserves the glorious crispiness that defines authentic Korean fried chicken, preventing it from becoming soggy.

## Serving Suggestions

Yangnyeom Korean Fried Chicken is a star on its own, but it truly shines when paired with traditional Korean accompaniments. The quintessential pairing is with a cold beer, a duo known as 'chimaek' (치맥). For a refreshing counterpoint to the rich, spicy chicken, serve it with 'mu-saengchae' (무생채), a simple pickled radish salad, or 'chikin-mu' (치킨무), cubed pickled radishes, which offer a delightful tangy crunch. Steamed white rice can also be served to soak up any extra sauce. For an extra touch, offer a side of cooling kimchi or a light green salad with a sesame dressing to balance the flavors.

jsonLd.name: Yangnyeom Korean Fried Chicken
jsonLd.description: Yangnyeom Korean Fried Chicken is a symphony of textures and flavors, featuring impossibly crispy chicken pieces coated in a sticky, sweet, spicy, and savory glaze. This beloved Korean dish offers a perfect balance of heat from gochujang, tang from vinegar, and richness from garlic and soy, making every bite an addictive experience.
jsonLd.keywords: Korean, Korean, authentic, traditional, chicken, Korean, authentic, traditional, chicken, spicy, fried chicken, yangnyeom
recipeIngredient: 2 lbs (about 1 kg) Chicken Wings and Drumettes (separated at the joint)
1 cup Potato Starch (or cornstarch for dredging)
1/2 cup Cold Water (or more, for batter consistency)
2 tbsp Gochugaru (Korean Chili Flakes) (adjust to desired spice level, for sauce)
1/4 cup Korean Rice Syrup (Mulyeot) (or corn syrup, for sauce)
4-6 cups Neutral Frying Oil (e.g., canola, vegetable, peanut)

recipeInstructions.text: Pat the chicken pieces thoroughly dry with paper towels. Cover and refrigerate for at least 20 minutes, or up to an hour, to allow flavors to meld.
